brief_justification: Answered Yes but correct answer is No — typical people distinguish proximate physical causes from distal lifestyle factors and do not attribute death to 'crime life' as a cause; Reasoning goes one level beyond surface by tracing the causal chain through multiple hops; Minimal self-monitoring — does not question whether folk causation actually works this way or consider empirical findings on how people judge such scenarios; Mentions mechanism validity checks but doesn't verify against how people actually respond to these vignettes; Presents folk causation claim with some hedging but no structured uncertainty; Briefly acknowledges proximate vs distal cause distinction but dismisses it without serious evaluation; Shows the causal chain derivation clearly but the key inference about typical person judgment is asserted rather than derived.
